new_value: {"description":"DESIGN CHOICE (not a bug): Polynomial partition function path (saha_boltzmann.py:179-185) uses pre-tabulated coefficients that already include natural IP truncation. When IPD lowers the continuum, the polynomial path does not adjust. This is a known approximation — polynomial coefficients cannot easily incorporate plasma-density-dependent IPD. The level-summation fallback path (lines 205-214) correctly applies IPD truncation. Consider: add a correction factor U_corrected = U_poly * (1 - correction(delta_chi)) or force level summation when IPD delta_chi \u003e threshold."}
old_value: {"id":"CF-LIBS-improved-6g8","title":"BUG: Partition function polynomial path ignores IPD lowered continuum","description":"In saha_boltzmann.py:179-185, when polynomial partition function coefficients exist, calculate_partition_function() returns immediately without applying max_energy_ev truncation. Only the level-summation path (lines 205-214) honors the lowered ionization potential. This means IPD affects the Saha exponential but NOT U(T) when polynomial coefficients are available, creating an inconsistency that biases ionization ratios. Fix: either apply an IPD correction factor to the polynomial PF, or always use level summation when IPD is active.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-21T17:35:01Z","created_by":"Brian Squires","updated_at":"2026-03-21T17:35:01Z"}

{"id":"CF-LIBS-improved-o2h","title":"BUG: self_absorption.py oscillator strength prefactor uses Angstrom-based constant with cm wavelength","description":"LATENT BUG (currently harmless): In self_absorption.py:819-825, the oscillator strength prefactor 1.4992e-16 is the Angstrom-based value but code uses lambda_cm. The formula gives f values 1e16 too small. However, this has NO practical effect because: (1) the actual optical depth calculation (line 424-426) uses empirical scaling 1e-25, not f_ik; (2) log_gf is only used in delta_log_gf (relative comparisons where the constant offset cancels). Fix the constant to 1.4992 for correctness, but this is cosmetic in the current code.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-21T17:35:01Z","created_by":"Brian Squires","updated_at":"2026-03-21T17:39:38Z"}

new_value: {"description":"LATENT BUG (currently harmless): In self_absorption.py:819-825, the oscillator strength prefactor 1.4992e-16 is the Angstrom-based value but code uses lambda_cm. The formula gives f values 1e16 too small. However, this has NO practical effect because: (1) the actual optical depth calculation (line 424-426) uses empirical scaling 1e-25, not f_ik; (2) log_gf is only used in delta_log_gf (relative comparisons where the constant offset cancels). Fix the constant to 1.4992 for correctness, but this is cosmetic in the current code."}
old_value: {"id":"CF-LIBS-improved-o2h","title":"BUG: self_absorption.py oscillator strength prefactor uses Angstrom-based constant with cm wavelength","description":"In self_absorption.py:819-825 and 1003-1006, the f_ik oscillator strength prefactor 1.4992e-16 is the Angstrom-based value (from classical oscillator strength formula). But the code multiplies it by lambda_cm^2 (wavelength in cm). The prefactor should be ~1.4992 (unitless) when using cm, or 1.4992e-16 when using Angstroms. This is a factor of ~1e16 error in the oscillator strength calculation used for self-absorption correction. The cdsb.py implementation at lines 168-170 uses nm correctly.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-21T17:35:01Z","created_by":"Brian Squires","updated_at":"2026-03-21T17:35:01Z"}

{"id":"CF-LIBS-improved-6g8","title":"BUG: Partition function polynomial path ignores IPD lowered continuum","description":"DESIGN CHOICE (not a bug): Polynomial partition function path (saha_boltzmann.py:179-185) uses pre-tabulated coefficients that already include natural IP truncation. When IPD lowers the continuum, the polynomial path does not adjust. This is a known approximation — polynomial coefficients cannot easily incorporate plasma-density-dependent IPD. The level-summation fallback path (lines 205-214) correctly applies IPD truncation. Consider: add a correction factor U_corrected = U_poly * (1 - correction(delta_chi)) or force level summation when IPD delta_chi \u003e threshold.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-21T17:35:01Z","created_by":"Brian Squires","updated_at":"2026-03-21T17:39:39Z"}

{"id":"CF-LIBS-improved-f37","title":"Add from __future__ import annotations to cflibs/inversion/boltzmann.py","description":"Add from __future__ import annotations as the first import after the module docstring. Then replace typing.List with list, typing.Optional with X | None, typing.Dict with dict, typing.Tuple with tuple. Run: ruff check cflibs/inversion/boltzmann.py --select UP006,UP007,FA100 to verify.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-21T16:47:28Z","created_by":"Brian Squires","updated_at":"2026-03-21T16:47:28Z"}

new_value: {"description":"GPU-accelerate Voigt profile computation in cflibs/radiation/profiles.py (833 LOC). Current: Humlicek W4 approximation computed per-line in a Python loop (lines ~200-350). Target: Replace the per-line loop with JAX vmap over all spectral lines, enabling GPU batch evaluation. Implementation: 1. Add jax import guard (like existing pattern in cflibs/manifold/): try/except ImportError with HAS_JAX flag 2. Create jax_voigt_batch(wavelengths, centers, sigma_g, sigma_l) using jax.scipy.special.erfc or custom W4 3. Wrap with @jax.jit for compilation caching 4. Use jax.vmap over the line dimension (axis=0) 5. Fallback to existing NumPy loop when JAX unavailable 6. Add unit test comparing JAX vs NumPy output (rtol=1e-6) Key constraint: Must preserve the existing API (VoigtProfile class interface). Expected speedup: 2-5x on V100 GPU."}

new_value: {"description":"JAX-vectorize Boltzmann plot fitting in cflibs/inversion/boltzmann.py (708 LOC). Current: BoltzmannPlotFitter.fit() runs a per-element Python loop with numpy.polyfit for weighted linear regression (lines ~300-450). Target: Vectorize across elements using JAX batched least-squares. Implementation: 1. Add jax/jnp import guard (same pattern as cflibs/manifold/) 2. Create _jax_batch_fit(x_arrays, y_arrays, weights) that vectorizes polyfit across elements via jax.vmap 3. Preserve outlier rejection (SIGMA_CLIP/RANSAC/HUBER) — run rejection in NumPy, then batch the final fit in JAX 4. Add jax_available() check: use JAX path when available, fall back to existing NumPy loop 5. Temperature extraction: T = -1/(kB * slope) stays the same 6. Add unit test: compare JAX batch fit vs sequential NumPy fit (rtol=1e-5) Key files: cflibs/inversion/boltzmann.py (main), cflibs/core/constants.py (KB_EV). Key constraint: BoltzmannPlotFitter API and BoltzmannFitResult dataclass must not change. Expected speedup: 5-10x for multi-element spectra."}

new_value: {"description":"Accelerate iterative solver convergence in cflibs/inversion/solver.py (714 LOC). Current: Simple fixed-point iteration with 10-20 iterations at ~50-200ms/spectrum (lines ~100-300). Each iteration: compute Saha correction factors, update temperature, check convergence. Target: Implement Anderson acceleration (mixing of last M iterates) to reduce iteration count by 40-60%. Implementation: 1. Add AndersonAccelerator class with configurable mixing depth M (default=5) 2. Store last M iterates and residuals in a ring buffer 3. Compute mixing coefficients via least-squares on residual matrix 4. Apply mixed update: x_{n+1} = sum(alpha_i * (x_i + f(x_i))) 5. Fall back to simple iteration if Anderson step increases residual (safeguard) 6. Optionally JIT-compile the inner Saha correction with JAX (when available) 7. Add convergence test comparing Anderson vs simple iteration count Key files: cflibs/inversion/solver.py (main), cflibs/plasma/state.py (PlasmaState). Key constraint: solve() API and convergence criteria must not change. Expected speedup: 2-4x (fewer iterations, JIT overhead amortized over batch)."}
old_value: {"id":"CF-LIBS-improved-hr8","title":"Accelerate iterative solver convergence with Anderson mixing","description":"cflibs/inversion/solver.py (714 LOC) uses 10-20 iterations for convergence at ~50-200ms/spectrum. Expected: 2-4x speedup.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-23T02:02:11Z","created_by":"Brian Squires","updated_at":"2026-03-23T02:02:11Z"}

new_value: {"description":"Enable GPU FAISS for manifold nearest-neighbor inference in cflibs/manifold/ (~1500 LOC). In the manifold query module, detect faiss-gpu availability: try faiss.get_num_gpus() 2. When GPU available: transfer IVFFlat index to GPU via faiss.index_cpu_to_gpu(res, 0, index) 3. Batch search: index.search(query_vectors, k) on GPU 4. Transfer results back to CPU for post-processing 5. Fallback to existing CPU FAISS when GPU unavailable 6. Add benchmark flag: --gpu to manifold CLI for explicit GPU selection 7. Add test: compare GPU vs CPU search results (exact match for IVFFlat with nprobe=nlist) Key constraint: ManifoldPredictor API must not change. HDF5/Zarr storage unchanged. new_value: {"description":"BUILD (Derived): GPU-accelerate Voigt profile in cflibs/radiation/profiles.py. GEMINI DEEP RESEARCH RECOMMENDATION: Do NOT use Zaghloul 2024 (causes GPU warp divergence due to branching). Instead, extract the branchless Faddeeva/Weideman formulation from ExoJAX opacity module (exojax.opacity). Implement as a pure jax.numpy function with @jax.jit. Implementation: 1. Study ExoJAX source: github.com/HajimeKawahara/exojax, specifically the opacity module Faddeeva implementation 2. Extract the branchless Weideman 32-term rational approximation (no if/else branches = no warp divergence) 3. Implement as pure jax.numpy function: jax_voigt(x, sigma, gamma) 4. Wrap with jax.vmap over the line dimension for batch evaluation 5. Add @jax.jit for XLA compilation caching 6. Fallback to existing NumPy Humlicek W4 when JAX unavailable 7. Unit test: compare JAX vs NumPy output (rtol=1e-6) Expected speedup: 50-100x on V100. Key constraint: VoigtProfile class API unchanged."}
old_value: {"id":"CF-LIBS-improved-z8u","title":"GPU-accelerate Voigt profile computation via JAX vmap","description":"GPU-accelerate Voigt profile computation in cflibs/radiation/profiles.py (833 LOC). Current: Humlicek W4 approximation computed per-line in a Python loop (lines ~200-350). Target: Replace the per-line loop with JAX vmap over all spectral lines, enabling GPU batch evaluation. Implementation: 1. Add jax import guard (like existing pattern in cflibs/manifold/): try/except ImportError with HAS_JAX flag 2. Create jax_voigt_batch(wavelengths, centers, sigma_g, sigma_l) using jax.scipy.special.erfc or custom W4 3. Wrap with @jax.jit for compilation caching 4. Use jax.vmap over the line dimension (axis=0) 5. Fallback to existing NumPy loop when JAX unavailable 6. Add unit test comparing JAX vs NumPy output (rtol=1e-6) Key constraint: Must preserve the existing API (VoigtProfile class interface). Expected speedup: 2-5x on V100 GPU.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-23T02:02:10Z","created_by":"Brian Squires","updated_at":"2026-03-23T14:37:28Z"}

new_value: {"description":"COMPOSE: JAX-vectorize Boltzmann fitting in cflibs/inversion/boltzmann.py. GEMINI DEEP RESEARCH RECOMMENDATION: Discard RANSAC and sigma-clipping for the JAX path — their dynamic array shapes conflict with XLA compilation (no dynamic shapes allowed in jit). Instead, compose Optax huber_loss with JAXopt batched solvers. Implementation: 1. pip install optax jaxopt (add to pyproject.toml optional deps) 2. Replace per-element numpy.polyfit loop with batched approach:    - Stack all elements x_arrays (E_k values) and y_arrays (ln(I*lambda/gA)) into padded tensors    - Use optax.huber_loss as the robust loss function (replaces sigma-clip/RANSAC/Huber M-est)    - Minimize with jaxopt.LBFGS or jaxopt.GradientDescent (batched across elements via vmap) 3. Temperature extraction: T_m = -1/(kB * slope_m) unchanged 4. Fallback: existing NumPy loop when JAX/optax unavailable 5. Unit test: compare JAX batch fit vs sequential NumPy fit (rtol=1e-5) Key insight: single compiled GPU tensor operation replaces iterative sequential CPU loops. Expected speedup: \u003e1000x. BoltzmannPlotFitter API and BoltzmannFitResult dataclass unchanged."}

new_value: {"description":"BUY: Use jaxopt.AndersonAcceleration for solver convergence in cflibs/inversion/solver.py. GEMINI DEEP RESEARCH RECOMMENDATION: Do NOT write custom Anderson acceleration. JAXopt already has a production-ready implementation. Structure the CF-LIBS inverse as a pure fixed-point function and pass to jaxopt.AndersonAcceleration. Implementation: 1. pip install jaxopt (add to pyproject.toml optional deps) 2. Refactor solve() inner loop as a pure function: g(x) -\u003e x where x = (T, n_e, C_vector)    - Must be a pure function (no side effects) for JAXopt compatibility    - The Saha correction, Boltzmann update, and closure are composed into g(x) 3. Call jaxopt.AndersonAcceleration(g, history_size=5, ridge=1e-6).run(x0)    - history_size=5 (M=5) per Gemini recommendation    - ridge parameter (Tikhonov regularization) critical for stability 4. Fallback: existing simple iteration when JAXopt unavailable 5. Test: compare iteration count and final T,n_e,C vs simple iteration Expected: 2-5x iteration reduction, convergence time \u003c1ms/spectrum (from 50-200ms). solve() API and convergence criteria unchanged."}

Add AndersonAccelerator class with configurable mixing depth M (default=5) 2. Store last M iterates and residuals in a ring buffer 3. Compute mixing coefficients via least-squares on residual matrix 4. Apply mixed update: x_{n+1} = sum(alpha_i * (x_i + f(x_i))) 5. Fall back to simple iteration if Anderson step increases residual (safeguard) 6. Optionally JIT-compile the inner Saha correction with JAX (when available) 7. Add convergence test comparing Anderson vs simple iteration count Key files: cflibs/inversion/solver.py (main), cflibs/plasma/state.py (PlasmaState). Key constraint: solve() API and convergence criteria must not change. Expected speedup: 2-4x (fewer iterations, JIT overhead amortized over batch).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"squires.b@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-03-23T02:02:11Z","created_by":"Brian Squires","updated_at":"2026-03-23T14:37:55Z"}

new_value: {"description":"BUY: Switch to faiss-gpu with brute-force IndexFlatL2 for manifold NN in cflibs/manifold/. GEMINI DEEP RESEARCH RECOMMENDATION: Use faiss-gpu v1.10.0+ which integrates NVIDIA cuVS (CUDA Vector Search). For our 3-5 dimensional dense parameter space, use IndexFlatL2 brute-force (NOT IVFFlat approximation) — exact results with GPU parallelism. Implementation: 1. pip install faiss-gpu (requires CUDA toolkit on vasp nodes) 2. In manifold query module, detect GPU: res = faiss.StandardGpuResources(); ngpus = faiss.get_num_gpus() 3. When GPU available: gpu_index = faiss.index_cpu_to_gpu(res, 0, cpu_index) 4. Key change: replace IVFFlat with IndexFlatL2 for exact brute-force on GPU    - For 3-5D with \u003c100M points, brute-force on GPU is faster than approximate search    - No training step needed (IndexFlatL2 is training-free) 5. Batch search: distances, indices = gpu_index.search(query_vectors, k=1) 6. Fallback: existing CPU FAISS when faiss-gpu unavailable 7. Test: compare GPU vs CPU results (must be identical for IndexFlatL2) Expected speedup: 10-50x. Microsecond-regime latency for batch queries. ManifoldPredictor API unchanged. HDF5/Zarr storage unchanged."}

new_value: {"description":"BUILD: Rewrite forward model as pure JAX pipeline in cflibs/radiation/batch.py + spectrum_model.py. GEMINI DEEP RESEARCH RECOMMENDATION: Systematically rewrite the NumPy/SciPy forward engine into pure jax.numpy. Emulate ExoJAX architectural patterns: @jax.jit for XLA kernel fusion, jax.vmap for batch parallelism across parameter states. Implementation: 1. Create jax_forward_model(T, n_e, C, wavelength_grid, line_db) as pure function    - All inputs as jax arrays (no Python objects, no dicts mid-computation)    - Chain: emissivity(T, n_e, C, line_db) -\u003e broadening(profiles, T) -\u003e convolution(spectrum, instrument) 2. Decorate with @jax.jit for compilation 3. Use jax.vmap(jax_forward_model, in_axes=(0,0,0,None,None)) for batch over plasma states    - T, n_e, C have batch dimension; wavelength_grid and line_db are shared 4. PlasmaState fields must be stacked into arrays: T_batch = jnp.array([s.T for s in states]) 5. Instrument convolution: jax.scipy.signal.fftconvolve or custom FFT-based 6. Fallback: existing ThreadPool/ProcessPool when JAX unavailable 7. Test: compare JAX batch vs sequential forward model output (rtol=1e-6) Key: bypasses Python GIL entirely. Operates at memory bandwidth limits. Expected speedup: orders of magnitude. batch_forward_model() API unchanged."}
